questions.question: Explain the difference between external and internal clock synchronization with an example.
keyPoints: External synchronization binds clocks to an external time source like UTC
Internal synchronization binds clocks to each other without external reference
Example: NTP provides external sync, Berkeley algorithm provides internal sync
External ensures global consistency, internal ensures consistency among machines only

questions.question: Describe Cristian's algorithm for clock synchronization with its limitations.
keyPoints: Client requests time from time server
Server responds with its current time
Client adds half of round-trip time to server's timestamp to estimate correct time
Maximum error equals half the round-trip time
Limitation: Assumes symmetric network delay, single point of failure if server fails

questions.question: What is clock drift? Explain how it affects distributed systems.
keyPoints: Clock drift is the rate at which a clock deviates from perfect time
Different clocks drift at different rates due to hardware variations
Causes clocks to become progressively out of sync
Requires periodic resynchronization in distributed systems
Can cause consistency issues in distributed databases and transaction ordering

questions.question: Explain the NTP stratum hierarchy and its significance.
keyPoints: Stratum 0: Primary reference clocks (atomic, GPS)
Stratum 1: Servers directly synchronized to Stratum 0
Stratum 2-15: Successive levels getting less accurate
Stratum 16: Unsynchronized, not used for sync
Significance: Provides scalable, hierarchical time synchronization and handles fault tolerance

questions.question: Compare Cristian's algorithm and Berkeley algorithm for clock synchronization.
keyPoints: Cristian: Client-server model, server is passive; Berkeley: Master-slave, master is active
Cristian adjusts to server time; Berkeley computes average of all clocks
Cristian is simpler; Berkeley is more fault-tolerant
Both assume symmetric network delays
Berkeley handles failed masters better

questions.question: What are the challenges in achieving perfect clock synchronization in distributed systems?
keyPoints: Unknown one-way message delay
Network delays are variable and unpredictable
Clock drift between machines
Hardware limitations of oscillators
No global clock in distributed systems
Security threats like malicious time servers

questions.question: Explain the working of the Berkeley algorithm for clock synchronization.
keyPoints: A master node is designated to control synchronization
Master polls all slave nodes for their clock times
Master computes the average of all times including its own
Master calculates adjustment values (difference from average)
Master sends adjustment values to all slaves
Slaves adjust their clocks accordingly

questions.question: Describe the Network Time Protocol (NTP) architecture and explain how it achieves clock synchronization across the internet. Include details about stratum levels, synchronization modes, and accuracy.
keyPoints: NTP uses hierarchical client-server architecture with stratum levels
Stratum 0: Primary reference clocks (atomic, GPS receivers)
Stratum 1: Primary servers directly connected to Stratum 0
Lower strata: Successive levels of secondary servers
Synchronization modes: Client-server, symmetric peer, broadcast
Uses selection and clustering algorithms to filter incorrect times
Provides accuracy of milliseconds on WAN, microseconds on LAN
Operates using UDP port 123
Four-message exchange for symmetric mode
Clients can use multiple servers for reliability

questions.question: Explain the concept of physical clocks vs logical clocks in distributed systems. Why is physical clock synchronization necessary? Discuss with examples of when physical clocks are preferred over logical clocks.
keyPoints: Physical clocks provide absolute time (UTC), logical clocks capture causal ordering
Logical clocks (Lamport) don't give real time, only 'happens-before' relationships
Physical clocks needed for: real-time applications, legal compliance, debugging, coordination with external systems
Examples: Stock trading, flight reservations, distributed databases requiring wall-clock ordering
Physical clocks face challenges: drift, network delays, asymmetric paths
Trade-off: Physical sync harder but necessary for real-world time requirements
Hybrid approaches combine both types for better accuracy

questions.question: A distributed system has three clocks with the following offsets from true time: Clock A = +5ms, Clock B = -3ms, Clock C = +8ms. If Cristian's algorithm is used with a time server having true time, and the round-trip time is 40ms, calculate the adjusted times for each clock and explain the synchronization process.
keyPoints: Half RTT = 40/2 = 20ms
Server time is true time T (say 1000ms)
Client receives T at their local time
Time correction = ServerTime + 20ms - ClientLocalTime
Clock A: Correction needed = +5ms - 20ms = -15ms (slow down)
Clock B: Correction needed = -3ms - 20ms = -23ms (slow down)
Clock C: Correction needed = +8ms - 20ms = -12ms (slow down)
Process: Client sends request → Server responds with timestamp → Client adds half RTT → Adjusts clock
Maximum error after sync = ±20ms (half RTT assumption)

questions.question: Discuss the Google True Time API used in Spanner database. How does it achieve clock synchronization and what are the guarantees provided? Explain how this differs from traditional NTP-based synchronization.
questions.trueTimeExplanation: True Time uses GPS receivers and atomic clocks in data centers to provide bounded uncertainty interval [earliest, latest] instead of a single time value, guaranteeing that true time lies within this interval.
keyPoints: True Time uses GPS and atomic clocks as reference
Provides confidence interval: TT.now() returns [earliest, latest]
True time guaranteed to be within this interval
Uses commit-wait protocol: wait until interval has passed
Difference from NTP: NTP gives single point estimate, True Time gives bounded uncertainty
NTP accuracy: milliseconds; True Time accuracy: single-digit milliseconds
True Time handles Byzantine failures of time servers
Enables globally distributed transactions with consistency
Uses Paxos for coordination with time bounds
